seasonal_wine.name: Winter pruning across the Beaujolais Crus, January to February
seasonal_wine.description: The Beaujolais winter belongs to pruning. Across the granite slopes of Morgon, Moulin-a-Vent, and Fleurie, growers work through the Gobelet-trained Gamay vines cutting back to two or three spurs. The region is quiet and unhurried, cellars still hold the just-bottled Cru releases, and domaine visits by appointment are relaxed and thorough.
what_to_taste: Just-bottled Cru Beaujolais
Library-release Morgon from the previous vintage
Barrel samples by appointment at natural-wine estates
seasonal_wine.tip: Winter is the best season for an unrushed visit to a small natural-wine domaine; growers have more time and the cellar temperature keeps wines at perfect tasting condition.

seasonal_wine.name: Saint Vincent feast day and local vine-grower celebrations, January
seasonal_wine.description: Saint Vincent, patron saint of winegrowers, is celebrated on 22 January across the Beaujolais Cru villages with modest local gatherings, communal tastings, and mass in the village church. Unlike the grand Burgundian Saint-Vincent Tournante, the Beaujolais celebrations remain intimate and village-specific, a living working-grower tradition.
what_to_taste: Village-poured Cru Gamay
Producers opening back-vintage bottles for the feast
seasonal_wine.tip: Ask your domaine contact whether they celebrate Saint Vincent; some Morgon and Fleurie growers open their cellars for a low-key tasting on or around 22 January.

seasonal_wine.name: Budbreak and spring frost watch, April
seasonal_wine.description: Gamay vines break bud in April, sometimes mid-month on the warmer Brouilly and Moulin-a-Vent slopes, and the frost threat that has hit the Beaujolais in recent years keeps growers anxious. Wind machines and smudge pots appear in susceptible parcels. The Pierres Dorées villages in the south and the low-lying Beaujolais-Villages zones are most vulnerable.
what_to_taste: Recently bottled village-level Beaujolais-Villages
Light Beaujolais blanc (Chardonnay) as a spring aperitif
seasonal_wine.tip: Spring shoulder season means sparse crowds; inquire about vineyard visits even at domaines that normally close outside harvest.

seasonal_wine.name: Fete des Crus Beaujolais, April
seasonal_wine.description: The annual Fete des Crus Beaujolais is organised by Inter Beaujolais each spring, typically in April, gathering producers from all ten Cru appellations for public tasting sessions in Villefranche-sur-Saone or rotating Cru villages. The event emphasises Cru identity over the Nouveau image and positions Beaujolais as a serious wine region for the spring export season.
what_to_taste: All ten Beaujolais Crus side by side
Latest vintage Fleurie, Morgon, and Moulin-a-Vent releases
Small-producer Beaujolais-Villages
seasonal_wine.tip: This is the best single event to taste across the Cru appellation spectrum in one session; book tickets via Inter Beaujolais when registration opens in February.

seasonal_wine.name: Flowering and fruit set, June
seasonal_wine.description: Gamay flowers in June and the brief flowering window sets the potential size of the harvest. Warm, dry flowering conditions give even fruit set and the prospect of a balanced vintage; cool or rainy June weather causes coulure (poor fruit set) and reduced yields. For the visitor, June is lush and green, with the canopy filling out and the Beaujolais countryside at its most photogenic.
what_to_taste: Previous vintage Cru releases settling into bottle
Beaujolais blanc (Chardonnay)
Imported summer sparklers at Beaujolais wine bars
seasonal_wine.tip: June visits are crowd-free and the vines are beautiful; natural-wine bars in Villié-Morgon and Fleurie are lively on summer evenings.

seasonal_wine.name: Veraison and heat-stress watch, August
seasonal_wine.description: Veraison, when the Gamay berries change colour from green to dark blue-black, typically falls in mid-August in the Beaujolais Crus. In recent hot vintages the speed of ripening through August has tested growers' timing; some natural-wine producers in Morgon and Fleurie now pick their oldest Gobelet vines in the first week of September to retain freshness.
what_to_taste: Summer Beaujolais by the glass at Lyon bistros
Chilled Gamay poured straight from the cellar at harvest-season estate visits
seasonal_wine.tip: Some estates now accept harvest volunteers in late August or early September; enquire directly with the domaine several months ahead.

seasonal_wine.name: Harvest season across the Crus, early September to early October
seasonal_wine.recurrence_pattern: annual, early September to early October depending on vintage
seasonal_wine.description: Harvest is the most atmospheric time to visit the Beaujolais. The ten Cru villages fill with picking crews, mobile fermentation tanks appear in courtyard cellar entrances, and the air carries the scent of crushed Gamay through Villié-Morgon, Fleurie, and Moulin-a-Vent. Whole-cluster carbonic maceration, the traditional Beaujolais method, fills caves with fermenting bunches of whole grapes for two to three weeks.
what_to_taste: Free-run jus de raisin before fermentation at welcoming estates
Previous-vintage Morgon and Moulin-a-Vent served at village bars
Harvest-table lunches
seasonal_wine.tip: The Beaujolais harvest runs earlier than Burgundy's; for a chance to see whole-cluster carbonic fermentation in action aim for the third week of September when the Cru cellars are at their most active.

seasonal_wine.name: Beaujolais Nouveau release, third Thursday of November
seasonal_wine.description: The third Thursday of November is the global Nouveau release. The Le Beaujolais Nouveau est arrive signal triggers simultaneous midnight unlocking parties from Tokyo and London to New York. In the Beaujolais itself the night is marked by the Sarmentelles de Beaujeu in the historic capital Beaujeu and by late-night pourings in Lyon bouchons. The wine is a carbonic-maceration Gamay drunk within weeks of harvest.
what_to_taste: Beaujolais Nouveau from multiple negociants and small producers
Beaujolais-Villages Nouveau as a step-up from basic Beaujolais
The contrast between a fresh Nouveau and a two-year-old Cru from the same grape
seasonal_wine.tip: The Sarmentelles de Beaujeu the night before release is the authentic local celebration; arrive in Beaujeu by the Wednesday evening to join the fire-and-procession event before the midnight pour.

seasonal_wine.name: Hospices de Beaujeu wine auction, mid-December
seasonal_wine.description: Modelled on the Hospices de Beaune charity auction in Burgundy, the Hospices de Beaujeu holds its annual auction of Beaujolais Cru barrels in December in the historic town of Beaujeu. Proceeds go to the local hospital. The auction is a much smaller affair than Beaune's but retains genuine local character and draws producers, merchants, and enthusiasts from across the region.
what_to_taste: Hospices de Beaujeu cuvée barrel samples
Current-vintage Cru Beaujolais poured at surrounding estate events
Beaujolais from the cellar beneath the Maison du Beaujolais
seasonal_wine.tip: Beaujeu is a genuinely historic Beaujolais town that rewards a visit beyond the auction; the Hôtel de Ville cellars and the narrow medieval streets are worth a half-day on any December visit.

seasonal_wine.name: Cru Beaujolais release window, spring of the following year
seasonal_wine.recurrence_pattern: annual, March to May following the harvest year
seasonal_wine.description: Unlike Nouveau, the ten Cru Beaujolais appellations typically release to market between March and May of the year following harvest. Morgon and Moulin-a-Vent producers who have used semi-carbonic or traditional maceration and aged in small-format oak or concrete may hold bottles until autumn of the year following harvest. The release window gives trade buyers and collectors a structured moment to buy young Crus.
what_to_taste: Newly released Fleurie, Chiroubles, Brouilly (lighter crus, earlier release)
Moulin-a-Vent and Morgon Cote du Py (fuller crus, later release)
First-pour cellar samples of just-bottled Cru
seasonal_wine.tip: The spring Cru release period overlaps with Inter Beaujolais press tastings; independent visitors can often join producers for informal tastings of the new vintage in March and April.

seasonal_wine.name: Inter Beaujolais press tasting and barrel sample season, March to April
seasonal_wine.description: Each spring Inter Beaujolais hosts a professional press and trade tasting of the new vintage Cru releases for wine journalists, importers, and buyers. The event has grown in prominence as critical interest in the Beaujolais Crus has increased through the 2010s and 2020s, attracting coverage from Decanter, Wine Advocate, Vinous, and the French specialist press.
what_to_taste: Structured flights of all ten Crus
Comparative vintage-to-vintage verticals at individual domaines
Natural-wine Cru barrel samples at Gang of Four-affiliated estates
seasonal_wine.tip: The professional tasting is trade- and press-only, but many producers hold their own open-cellar days during the same March week; check individual domaine calendars for open-tastings aligned with the Inter Beaujolais press week.

seasonal_wine.name: Beaujolais Marathon des Vignes, autumn
seasonal_wine.description: The Beaujolais Marathon des Vignes is a popular annual running event that routes participants through Cru vineyard paths between Moulin-a-Vent, Fleurie, Chenas, and Julienas, with wine stations along the route. It is one of the most attended wine-themed sporting events in France and draws participants from across Europe for a weekend of wine, running, and Cru tastings.
what_to_taste: Cru Beaujolais poured at vineyard stations along the marathon route
Post-race Moulin-a-Vent and Fleurie at the finish village
Local charcuterie and cheese with wine at the village market
seasonal_wine.tip: Register months ahead as places fill quickly; accommodation in Moulin-a-Vent, Fleurie, and Chenas books out for the marathon weekend well in advance.
